explanation_md: `p` and `q` both point to the same heap-allocated `int`. Writing `*q = 99` modifies that object, so reading `*p` afterwards yields `99`. They are two pointers to one location, not two separate copies.

options.a: The block is freed and `p` is a null pointer — safe to use `p` as a guard
options.b: The block is freed, but `p` still points to the old address
options.c: Setting `p = nullptr` automatically frees the block
options.d: The block remains allocated until the program exits

explanation_md: `delete p` frees the heap block. Then `p = nullptr` makes `p` a null pointer, so any subsequent dereference (`*p`) will crash visibly rather than silently reading freed memory. Option b describes what happens without the null assignment; c and d are both false.

options.a: The pointer `p` is never set to `nullptr` after `delete`
options.b: `delete` is called on a stack variable, which is undefined behavior
options.c: `x` should be declared `const` before taking its address
options.d: The program prints `done` but should print `7`

explanation_md: `x` lives on the stack, not the heap. `delete` must only be used on memory returned by `new`. Passing a stack address to `delete` is undefined behavior — the heap's bookkeeper has no record of that address. Option a describes a style recommendation, not the actual bug here.
`*a` is initialised to 1 and `*b` to 2. `*a += *b` adds 2 into the location `a` points to, making `*a == 3`. Then `b` is freed (and nulled), and `*a` is printed before `a` is freed.

prompt_md: Which of the following best describes a **memory leak**?

options.a: Reading from memory after it has been `delete`d
options.b: Allocating heap memory with `new` and never calling `delete` on that pointer
options.c: Declaring more local variables than the stack can hold
options.d: Calling `delete` on the same pointer twice

explanation_md: A memory leak occurs when heap memory is allocated but the corresponding `delete` is never called, so the block is never returned to the system. Option a describes a use-after-free, option c describes a stack overflow, and option d describes a double-delete.
